text:
University students should generally be required to attend classes because regular attendance strengthens learning, accountability, and academic community. Although independent study is important, most students benefit from structured contact with lecturers and classmates. In my view, attendance should remain a core expectation, especially in courses that depend on discussion, feedback, and progressive skill building.

First, attending class improves understanding because students receive explanations that go beyond textbooks and slides. A lecturer can clarify difficult theories, answer questions immediately, and adjust examples when students seem confused. This real-time support helps learners avoid misunderstandings that may continue for weeks if they study alone. In addition, face-to-face lessons encourage students to keep pace with the course. When attendance is optional, many young adults postpone work until assessments approach, which often leads to shallow learning and unnecessary stress. It also creates a steady habit of preparation and review that helps complex ideas remain in long-term memory.

Second, classes develop communication and collaboration skills that cannot be fully learned in isolation. University is not only a place to absorb information; it is also an environment where students practice debating ideas, listening respectfully, and working with people from different backgrounds. Seminars, laboratory sessions, and group presentations all prepare students for professional life. These experiences lose much of their value when students participate irregularly.

Admittedly, there are cases in which flexibility is reasonable. Mature students, people with disabilities, and those balancing employment may need alternative arrangements. However, exceptions should not become the general rule. Universities can provide recorded lectures or hybrid options while still treating attendance as the standard expectation.

In conclusion, class attendance should usually be compulsory because it supports stronger academic performance, better discipline, and richer social learning. Optional attendance may sound attractive, but it often weakens the educational experience for both individuals and the wider class.

I believe governments should spend more money on public transport than on building additional roads, although both areas matter for a modern economy. Public transport helps large numbers of people travel efficiently, reduces pollution, and can improve equality for citizens who cannot afford private vehicles.

One strong reason for prioritising buses, trains, and metro systems is that they move many people at the same time. A single train can replace hundreds of cars on the road, which means less traffic congestion during busy hours. In large cities, simply adding more roads does not always solve transport problems because new road space often becomes crowded very quickly. Better public transport, by contrast, gives commuters a realistic alternative to driving.

Another advantage is environmental. Private cars contribute heavily to air pollution and carbon emissions, especially when traffic is slow. If governments invest in reliable and affordable public transport, more people may choose it voluntarily. This shift can improve public health and help cities meet climate targets. It can also reduce noise and make urban areas more pleasant places to live. Well-designed transport networks can even support local business activity around stations and major routes.

Public transport investment also has a social benefit. Students, elderly people, and low-income workers often depend on buses or trains. When those services are safe and frequent, people can reach jobs, schools, and hospitals more easily. Roads mostly benefit car owners, while transport networks can support a wider section of society.

However, I do not think governments should ignore roads completely. Some regional areas depend on them, and freight transport still requires good highways. Nevertheless, if a government must choose where to place greater emphasis, public transport is the wiser long-term investment because it addresses congestion, sustainability, and access at the same time.

There is a lot of discussion today about whether employees are more productive when they work from home or when they go to an office. Both ideas have advantages and disadvantages, and I think the best answer depends on the kind of work and the person doing it.

Working from home can improve productivity for several reasons. First, people save time because they do not need to travel to work every day. This extra time can be used for starting tasks earlier or getting more rest, which may help concentration. Also, home can be quieter than an office for some workers, especially if the office has many meetings and interruptions. As a result, employees may finish individual tasks faster. Many workers also appreciate having more control over their daily schedule.

On the other hand, office work also has important benefits. In an office, communication is often quicker because workers can ask questions directly and solve problems together. Team projects may move faster when people are in the same place. New staff also learn more easily by observing experienced colleagues. These points show that offices still play a useful role.

However, working from home is not always easy. Some people have children, noise, or limited space at home, so they cannot focus well. Also, when workers stay at home too much, they can feel isolated and less connected to their team. This may reduce motivation over time.

In my opinion, neither system is perfect by itself. A mixed approach is probably the most productive because it combines flexibility with collaboration. Employees can do focused tasks at home and attend the office for meetings, training, and teamwork. Therefore, productivity is highest when companies choose a balanced system instead of following only one method.

Some people say children should learn foreign language in primary school, but others think later is better. I think early learning have some good points, however it also bring some problems and schools should be careful how they do it.

Firstly, young children can copy sounds easier than older students. They are not shy and they like songs, games and repeat words many times. Because of this, they may get better pronunciation and more confidence in speaking. Also, if they start early, they have more years for practice so maybe they become fluent faster. This is why many parents feel excited about an early start.

But there are disadvantages too. In primary school, childrens already need study math, reading, writing and science. Adding one more subject can make pressure for them and teachers. In many schools there is not enough trained teachers also, so classes may become simple memorising and not real communication. If the teaching quality is low, early start does not always give good result.

Another issue is that some parents want children focus on mother tongue first. This idea is understandable because strong basic literacy is very important. If students confuse grammar rules between two languages, they maybe feel frustrated. Still, I do not think this is a major reason to delay every child.

In conclusion, starting a foreign language in primary school is generally better because young learners can absorb pronunciation and vocabulary more naturally. However, the program must be designed in a light and interesting way. Schools should use games and speaking activities instead of only written exercises. If schools only add extra homework and boring lessons, the benefit will be small and students can lose motivation quickly.

Government spend money for science is not always good because many people need help now. I think social needs is more important mostly and research can wait sometime. For many families, food price, rent, and medical care are already difficult, so they do not feel big science budget is the first priority.

Science make new things and that is useful, but many project take long time and normal person dont see result. If hospital is bad or people have no house, spending big money on laboratory look wrong. The government should fixing daily problem first because citizen living there every day. When leaders talk about future invention but streets are unsafe and clinics are full, public trust can become lower.

Also, some research is very expensive and maybe fail. When it fail, money is lost and no benefit come. This is risky for poor country. They need school, transport, food support and jobs. Scientist can still work but not too much budget. In developing nations, the same amount of money could build classrooms, improve water systems, or support unemployed workers for months.

However, science is not completely unnecessary. Medicine research help disease and technology can improve farming or clean energy. But these things should be only when main social issue already under control. If government make balance, maybe it is okay. I think small and useful research is acceptable, especially when it has direct result for health, agriculture, or disaster prevention.

In conclusion, I disagree that government should spend more on scientific research than other social need. Research is important but people basic life problem is first and must solve before other ambition plan. If a country ignore poverty and public service, then scientific success will not feel meaningful for ordinary citizens.
